coredata.dc:title: A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Ya Jie
Wang, Zhen Guang
Su, Ming
Liu, Xiao Tong
Shen, Shi Gang
Dong, Jiang Xue
coredata.dc:description: The widespread presence of tetracyclines in the environment has raised concerns about the potential risks to ecosystems and human health. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting tetracyclines was developed using europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) as probes under alkaline conditions. The sensing mechanism of sensor for tetracyclines was considered as inner filter effect (IFE), antenna effect (AE), and self-quenching effect (SQE). The sensor had a wide linear detection range than the reported europium ions-based tetracyclines sensors. The linear detection ranges of oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and chlorotetracycline (CTC) were respectively 0.00–603.75 μM, 0.00–623.82 μM, 0.00–594.61 μM and 0.00–601.54 μM, and the corresponding detection limits were respectively 9.50 nM, 15.80 nM, 10.40 nM and 90.30 nM. The smartphone with RGB Color Picker was further employed to analyze the concentration of tetracyclines, which provided a new method for visual tetracyclines detection. The application of Eu-CDs test paper was also explored, and the results showed that the Eu-CDs test paper has great potential application in the visual detection of tetracyclines. In addition, the accuracy of the established tetracyclines sensor was compared with that of the China national standard method by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the results showed that the established method in this work has similar accuracy to the China national standard method. The sensor has been employed to detect tetracyclines in the actual samples with satisfactory results, which indicated that this method has promising applications in the real-time monitoring tetracyclines of food and environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Colorimetric
Tetracyclines
Visual quantitation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of tetracyclines with a ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Visualization and rapid detection of tetracyclines

Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

Fundation for Central Government Guidance for Local Science and Technology Development of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. B2019201397 , B2015201193 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M670678 ), the Fundation for Central Government Guidance for Local Science and Technology Development of Hebei Province (No. 216Z2605G ), Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Hebei Province- High Level Talent Team Construction Project (No. 225A4802D ), Science and Technology Innovation Bureau of Baoding High-tech Zone Unveiled the Top Special Project (No. JBGS-2022-1-011 ), the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of Hebei University (No. 521000981136 ), Research Innovation Team of College of Chemistry and Materials Science of Hebei University (No. hxkytd-py2101 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic diagram of preparation of Eu-CDs. (b) The emission spectra of Eu-CDs, and insets were the photos of Eu-CDs solution under daylight (i) and 365 nm UV lamps (ii). (c) and (d) were HRTEM images of Eu-CDs, and the inset of (c) showed the size distribution of Eu-CDs. (e) The FT-IR spectrum of Eu-CDs. (f) XPS full-scan spectrum of Eu-CDs. XPS spectra of (g) C1s, (h) N1s, (j) O1s, and (k) S2p of Eu-CDs.

(a) and (b) were the emission spectra of Eu-CDs, adding various concentrations of OTC, Insets of (a) were the photos of the corresponding reaction system under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamps (bottom). (c)The ultraviolet absorption spectra of Eu-CDs, OTC, Eu-CDs + OTC and excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs. (d) The fluorescence lifetime of Eu-CDs at 450 nm with adding various concentrations of OTC. (e) Schematic diagram of sensing mechanism.

(a) and (e) Linear relationship of I617/I450 with the concentration of OTC and TC, respectively. (c) and (d) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs after adding various concentrations of OTC. (g) and (h) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs after adding various concentrations of TC. (b) and (f) Corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram. (i) and (j) Corresponding photos under 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) and (b) I617/I450 of the reaction system with OTC, TC, and interfering substances. (c) Corresponding photos under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamps (bottom). The concentration of Ca2+ and Cu2+ was 3.12 mM, the concentration of Ery was 1.56 mM, and the concentration of Fe3+ was 312 μM. The concentrations of CTC, TC, DC and OTC were 601.54 μM, 603.75 μM, 594.61 μM and 623.82 μM, respectively, and the concentration of other interfering substances was 6.24 mM.

(a) and (b) Photos of Eu-CDs test paper under daylight and UV lamps, respectively. (c) Photos of Eu-CDs test paper after adding different concentrations of OTC (0.00, 2.01, 10.06, 100.63, 201.25, 402.50, 805.00, 1006.25, 1408.75 and 2012.50 μM). (d) Photos of the filter paper under UV lamp. (e) Photos of the filter paper after absence and presence of different concentrations of OTC (0.00, 10.06, 20.13, 50.31, 100.63, 201.25, 503.13, 1006.25, 1509.38 and 1811.25 μM) under UV lamp.
Schematic diagram of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform for quantitatively detecting OTC.

(a) and (b) Comparison the proposed method and the China national standard method for a known concentration of OTC solution and for the addition of OTC standard solution to the actual samples, respectively.

head.ce:title: A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Wusi Road, Baoding, 071002, People's Republic of China.
Corresponding author. Wusi Road, Baoding, 071002, People's Republic of China..

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread presence of tetracyclines in the environment has raised concerns about the potential risks to ecosystems and human health. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting tetracyclines was developed using europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) as probes under alkaline conditions. The sensing mechanism of sensor for tetracyclines was considered as inner filter effect (IFE), antenna effect (AE), and self-quenching effect (SQE). The sensor had a wide linear detection range than the reported europium ions-based tetracyclines sensors. The linear detection ranges of oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and chlorotetracycline (CTC) were respectively 0.00–603.75 μM, 0.00–623.82 μM, 0.00–594.61 μM and 0.00–601.54 μM, and the corresponding detection limits were respectively 9.50 nM, 15.80 nM, 10.40 nM and 90.30 nM. The smartphone with RGB Color Picker was further employed to analyze the concentration of tetracyclines, which provided a new method for visual tetracyclines detection. The application of Eu-CDs test paper was also explored, and the results showed that the Eu-CDs test paper has great potential application in the visual detection of tetracyclines. In addition, the accuracy of the established tetracyclines sensor was compared with that of the China national standard method by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the results showed that the established method in this work has similar accuracy to the China national standard method. The sensor has been employed to detect tetracyclines in the actual samples with satisfactory results, which indicated that this method has promising applications in the real-time monitoring tetracyclines of food and environment.

A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor was developed based on multicolor carbon dots as probes with smartphone-assisted visual assay.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines, including OTC, TC, DC, CTC, and so on, have been widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture industry on account of low cost, excellent antibacterial effect, and promotion of animal growth [1–3]. However, overuse of tetracyclines has resulted in residues in livestock and fish, and is widely present in soil, surface water, groundwater, and drinking water [4]. Both high tetracyclines levels and chronic low tetracyclines levels can create selective pressure on pathogens, leading to the spread of antibiotic-resistance genes, which may pose a threat to ecosystem [5–7]. Moreover, studies indicated that continuous ingestion of tetracyclines from food could cause some health problems, for example, anaphylaxis, gastrointestinal disorders, and liver damage [8–10]. Hence, it is crucial to establish a simple, rapid method for sensitively detecting tetracyclines in the fields of food safety and environmental protection.

Up to now, various methods for detecting tetracyclines have been reported, for example, solid phase extraction-high performance liquid chromatography [11], chemiluminescence immunoassay [12], electrochemistry [13], colorimetry [14,15] and fluorometry [16–19], etc. Among them, fluorimetry is widely applied to various fields because of its low cost, easy operation, small sample dose, high sensitivity, rapid response, and visual analysis [20]. Related studies have shown that the single emission of fluorescent probes is susceptible to external environmental influences, such as light scattering from the sample and photobleaching of the fluorophore. Dual-emission fluorescent probes could break through this limitation to achieve self-calibration, more importantly, multicolor changes for visual monitoring of the targets [21–23]. The visual detection of targets relies heavily on our naked eyes, and it cannot distinguish tiny color changes. Recently, fluorimetry based on smartphone-assisted visual assay has been gradually established [24], Han et al. used a smartphones-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for visual detection of TC [22]; Wang et al. used a colorimetric fluorescence sensor and a smartphone for visual detection of cadmium ions [23]; Thus, fluorimetry integrated with smartphones is a new method for fast and visual targets detection.

As our previous research, tetracyclines have a wide absorption band in the wavelength ranges of 220–430 nm [25], thus, when the excitation or emission wavelength of the fluorescent probes are located in the range of 220–430 nm, the fluorescence of the probes will be quenched because of IFE, and developing and establishing a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for tetracyclines is very difficult. Furthermore, because tetracyclines contain a β-diketone configuration, they could be used as a sensitizer to react with Eu3+ through the AE, therefore, the Eu3+-tetracyclines complex fluorometric method can be applied to detect trace amounts of tetracyclines. However, the fluorescence of Eu3+ can be quenched by coordinated water, resulting in poor sensitivity to tetracyclines [26,27]. To improve the sensitivity, Eu3+ usually combined with nanomaterials as probes for tetracyclines detection [21,29,30]. Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, can be modified by metal ions because the surface of CDs have the abundance of carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl, and amino groups [31,32]. Therefore, europium combined with carbon dots could be used as probes to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors without background interference compared to CDs, and it possess a remarkable color change, avoiding the disadvantage of single-emission fluorescent probes form a single color susceptible to interference. The complex contained Eu3+ and CDs have been reported to establish ratiometric fluorescent sensor for tetracyclines detection. For example, Hu and cooperator used a mixture of carbon dots and cytidine monophosphate/europium as probes to detect tetracycline with a narrow detection range (0.05–30 μM) [28]. Jia and the participator established a tetracycline sensor using carbon dots and Eu3+, but the preparation process of probes is cumbersome and the detection range of target is only from 0.025 to 20 μM [29]. Sang and coworkers used europium-doped carbon dots as probes for detecting tetracycline under weak alkaline conditions, but the result showed that effect of Al3+ on the sensor is inevitable under weak alkaline conditions [33] because tetracyclines can react with various metal ions (such as Ca2+, Mg2+, and Al3+) under weak acid, neutral, and slightly alkaline conditions [19,33–35]. Therefore, developing the simple preparation methods of Eu-CDs and highly selective tetracyclines sensors to avoid the interference of metal ions are of great challenge.

In this work, Eu-CDs were prepared by the simple one-pot method, and the fluorescence intensity of prepared Eu-CDs is stable in the wide range from pH 4 to pH 12, thus the prepared Eu-CDs were used as ratiometric probes under alkaline conditions to detect tetracyclines and avoid the interference of metal ions. The sensing mechanisms was attributed to IFE, AE and SQE, and the developed tetracyclines ratiometric fluorescent sensor has the strengths of good anti-interference ability, high sensitivity and wide linear detection range. Furthermore, Eu-CDs were employed as probes for the detection of tetracyclines by colorimetric assay (OTC as the representative of tetracyclines). A visual detection platform for tetracyclines was established utilizing a smartphone and a portable UV lamp, providing a new method for the visual detection of tetracyclines. Moreover, the application of the Eu-CDs test paper was also explored, and the results showed that the Eu-CDs test paper has great potential for visual tetracyclines detection. The accuracy of the established method was compared with the China national standard method (GB/T 5009.116–2003) for detecting tetracyclines by HPLC, and the results indicated that the established method has similar accuracy to the China national standard method. The practicality of the sensor was explored, and the sensor was successfully used to detect tetracyclines in the actual samples.

The Eu-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal method, and reaction temperature and time were consistent with our previous work [25]. Eu(NO3)3•6H2O (0.6691 g), citric acid (CA, 0.4803 g), N, N′-Diethylthiourea (0.9917 g), and 20 mL deionized water were added to the beaker, the above mixture was transferred to microwave digestion tank and placed in a constant temperature oven (170 °C) for 12 h to obtain the Eu-CDs solution. Then, 15 mL deionized water was added into the cooling Eu-CDs solution. The diluted Eu-CDs solution was centrifuged with 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove large or agglomerated particles, and repeating the above process three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracyclines with a ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The tetracyclines were detected follow the steps as below: Britton-Robinson (BR, 100 μL 0.04 M, pH = 11) buffer solution, a series of OTC (1g mL−1), TC (1g mL−1), DC (1g mL−1) and CTC (1g mL−1) solutions with different volumes, and 9 μL of Eu-CDs solution were added successively to deionized water, and the final volume of each solution was 1 mL. The reaction solutions were aged for 5 min at room temperature, then the fluorescence spectra were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Besides, the BR buffer solution was got by adjusting the pH of 0.04 M mixed acid solution (phosphoric acid, boric acid, and acetic acid) with 0.02 M NaOH through the pH meter.

Firstly, 90 μL of Eu-CDs and 1 mL of BR (0.04 M, pH = 11) buffer solution were added to 8.91 mL of deionized water and mixed thoroughly. Then, the prepared circular filter paper was put in the above mixed solution for 1 min. Finally, the above filter paper was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, Eu-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. The as-prepared Eu-CDs solution was pale yellow under a daylight lamp and emitted a great blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1b), moreover, Eu-CDs exhibited a strong excitation-dependent and the emission wavelengths will red-shift with increasing excitation wavelengths, and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 370 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the morphology and size of Eu-CDs were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). HRTEM image exhibited a large dispersion of Eu-CDs with an average diameter of 4 nm (inset of Fig. 1c). In addition, the surface functional groups of Eu-CDs were explored by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Fig. 1e). The broad band at 3432 cm−1 was verified as the N–H and O–H stretching vibration [36,37]. The band located at 2985 cm−1 was considered a stretching vibration of C–H [38]. The peak at 1384 cm−1 was verified as the bending vibration of C–O, while the peaks at 1694 cm−1, 1561 cm−1, 1248 cm−1 and 1134 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of C=O, –COO, C–N, and C–O, respectively [39–42]. This indicated that the surface of the prepared Eu-CDs has the abundance of –OH, –C=O, –COOH and –NH. The surface elemental and chemical bonds of Eu-CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Eu-CDs were consist of C, N, O, S, and Eu elements with corresponding binding energies of 283.2 eV, 398.4 eV, 530.3 eV, 161.8 eV, and 1133.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 1f). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1g) showed the surface of Eu-CDs has the C–C (284.8 eV), C–N/C–O (286.1 eV), and COO− (288.4 eV) on [21,43,44]. The fitted spectrum of N1s (Fig. 1h) indicated the surface of Eu-CDs has the C–N (399.8 eV), and N–H (400.6 eV) [36]. The high-resolved spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1j) revealed the surface of Eu-CDs present C=O/C–O (531.9 eV) and O–H (533.5 eV) [45]. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 1k) exhibited the surface of Eu-CDs present Sn 2- (161.9 eV), S2p3/2 (163.5 eV)/S2p1/2(165.4 eV) and S2O3 2- (168.5 eV) [46]. The above results further suggested that the surface of Eu-CDs possessed a number of -OH, –C=O, –COOH and –NH, which supported the results of FT-IR spectrum. In a follow-up study, the stability of the prepared Eu-CDs was investigated, the change in the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs was tested with storage time, and the results indicated that the fluorescent property of Eu-CDs could be stable for at least 30 days at 4 °C refrigerator (Fig. S1). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Eu-CDs was investigated by direct measurement, and the fluorescence QY of Eu-CDs was 8.79%. The detailed method for measuring fluorescence QY was placed in the Supplementary Material.

As shown in Fig. 2a and b , the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 450 nm gradually decreased with the increase of OTC concentration. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 617 nm firstly increased and then decreased with the increase of OTC concentration. Insets of Fig. 2a were the photos of the corresponding reaction system under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamps (bottom). As shown in insets of Fig. 2a, the color of the reaction system gradually changed from blue to red as the concentration of OTC increased. Subsequently, the possible sensing mechanism for detecting tetracyclines was discussed and explored with the OTC as the model of tetracyclines. As shown in Fig. 2c, it is not appearance new absorption peaks in the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of Eu-CDs + OTC compared to Eu-CDs and OTC, indicating that it was not produced new chemicals during the reaction. Furthermore, the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of OTC and the excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs overlapped heavily, and it is possible that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE caused the fluorescence quenched of Eu-CDs at 450 nm. During FRET, the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore decreases due to the energy transferred from the fluorophore to the quencher, while the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore was not changed when the fluorescence of the fluorophore was quenched by the IFE-based detection substance [21,47]. To further illustrate the sensing mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime (450 nm) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence lifetime of the reaction system was almost unchanged after adding different concentrations of OTC into the system. Therefore, the fluorescence of Eu-CDs at 450 nm was quenched by OTC, primarily due to IFE. The red fluorescence of Eu-CDs originated from Eu3+, the emission peaks at 617 nm were thought to be the 5D0→7F2 transitions of Eu3+, OTC enhanced the fluorescence of Eu-CDs at 617 nm through AE with the assistance of –COOH [22,42,48]. However, when the tetracyclines exceeded a critical concentration, the red fluorescence of Eu-CDs was gradually quenched with increasing tetracyclines concentrations. Here, the red fluorescence quenching of Eu-CDs was considered as the SQE of a high concentration of the fluorescent substance. Accordingly, Eu-CDs were used as probes to establish ratiometric sensors for detecting tetracyclines in solution. A schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism is shown in Fig. 2e.

To obtain the good sensing performance of the established sensor for detecting tetracyclines, some reaction conditions were optimized, including the pH value, reaction time, and the dosage of probes. First, the pH value was optimized, as shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs could remain stable in the range of pH 4 to pH 12, and the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system (Eu-CDs + OTC, OTC was chosen as the representative) at 617 nm had the maximum at pH 11 (Fig. S3a), and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I450) also had the maximum at pH 11; hence, the optimal reaction pH value was pH 11 (Fig. S3b). Then, the reaction time was studied (Fig. S3c and Fig. S3d), and the results indicated that the reaction can be completed in 5 min after adding OTC into the reaction system. As mentioned above, the probes dosage played a vital role in the detection range, and Fig. S3e and Fig. S3f showed that there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and probes dosage in the range of 1–9 μL mL−1. Therefore, 9 μL mL−1 was chosen as the optimal probes dosage.

The performance of Eu-CDs for detecting tetracyclines was investigated under the above optimum experimental conditions. There were good linear relationships between I617/I450 and OTC concentration in the range of 0.00–603.75 μM with a detection limit of 9.50 nM, and the linear equation was I617/I450 = 0.01981 [OTC] – 0.44937, R = 0.9913 (Fig. 3 a); The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram showed the color of corresponding reaction systems. As shown in the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3b), the color of the reaction systems gradually from blue to red with the increasing OTC concentration, and the color of reaction systems change was consistent with the corresponding photos (Fig. 3i). As shown in Fig. 3c, the linear detection range of the established OTC sensor was 0.00–80.50 μM. When the OTC exceeded 80.50 μM, the red fluorescence of Eu-CDs was gradually quenched with increasing OTC concentrations, and there was still a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I450) and OTC, the linear detection range of the established OTC sensor was 0.00–603.75 μM in this case. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 617 nm firstly increased and then decreased with the increase of OTC concentration (Fig. 3c and d), which make the established tetracyclines sensor possess the large detection range in this study. At the same time, a good linear relationship between I617/I450 and TC concentrations could be observed from 0.00 to 623.82 μM with the detection limit of 15.80 nM, and the linear equation was I617/I450 = 0.01194 [TC] – 0.18108, R = 0.9934 (Fig. 3e). From the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3f), the color of reaction systems gradually from blue to red with TC concentration increased, and the color of reaction systems change was consistent with the corresponding photos (Fig. 3j). As shown in Fig. 3g and h, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 617 nm firstly increased and then decreased with the increase of TC concentration, and the red fluorescence of Eu-CDs was gradually increased with the increasing TC concentration in the range of 0.00–51.99 μM. While fluorescence intensity at 617 nm decreased with the increasing TC concentration when the TC concentration exceeded 51.99 μM, and there was still a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I450) and TC, thus the linear detection range of the established TC sensor was 0.00–623.82 μM. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3i and j, the color of reaction systems changed significantly from blue, sky blue, milk white, pink to red with increasing the concentration of OTC or TC; this also indicated that OTC or TC could be detected semi-quantitatively by the naked eye with the assistance of 365 nm UV lamp.

In addition, the sensor performance for detecting DC or CTC was further investigated (Fig. S4).When the DC or CTC were added into the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 450 nm gradually decreased with the increase of DC or CTC concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system at 617 nm firstly increased and then decreased with the increasing DC or CTC concentrations (Fig. S4a and Fig. S4c). As shown in Fig. S4b and Fig. S4d, the linear detection ranges of the established sensor for DC and CTC were 0.00–594.61 μM and 0.00–601.54 μM, respectively, and the corresponding linear equation were I617/I450 = 0.01819 [DC] – 0.00254 (R = 0.9906) and I617/I450 = 0.00209 [CTC] + 0.02568 (R = 0.9944) with the detection limits of 10.40 nM and 90.30 nM, respectively.

Under the optimized reaction conditions (OTC as the representative of tetracyclines), OTC was detected by colorimetric methods. As shown in Fig. S5a, the absorbance of the reaction system at 390 nm gradually increased with increasing OTC concentration. A good linear relationship between A (390 nm) and OTC concentration can be observed from 0.00 to 120.75 μM (Fig. S5b and Fig. S5c). The corresponding photos of Eu-CD solution after adding various concentrations of OTC under visible light were shown in Fig. S5d, where the color of the reaction system gradually deepens with increasing OTC concentration. These results suggest that tetracyclines could be detected by fluorometric and colorimetric methods.

The selectivity of the sensor was explored, as well as the effects of chloramphenicol (Chl), erythromycin (Ery), and some potential interfering substances (l-cysteine (L-Cys), Thiamine (VB1), vitamin B6 (VB6), L-histamine (L-His), l-glutamine (L-Glu), L-glutathione (L-GSH), l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), D-(+)-glucose (D-(+)-Glc), L(+)-ascorbic acid (L(+)-VC), CA, PO4 3-, CO3 2-, HCO3 −, F−, SO4 2-, Cl−, NO3 −, CH3COO−, C2O4 2-, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Ni2+) were explored. As shown in Fig. 4a and b , the I617/I450 of the reaction system was nearly unchanged, containing the above-mentioned interfering substances. Fig. 4c showed the corresponding photos under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamps (bottom). The color of the reaction solution changed from pale yellow to bright yellow under a daylight lamp after adding TC, DC or OTC into the reaction system, and the fluorescent color of reaction solution changed from blue to red under a 365 nm UV lamp. When concentrations of CTC, DC, TC and OTC are comparable, the color of the reaction solution changed from pale yellow to deep yellow under a daylight lamp after adding CTC into the reaction system, meanwhile, the fluorescent color of reaction solution changed from blue to purple under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the I617/I450 value is obviously lower after adding CTC into the reaction system compared to DC, TC and OTC (Fig. 4a). The above results indicated that prepared probes could distinguish CTC from DC, TC and OTC under experimental conditions, and above results also showed that the established sensor has excellent selectivity, and strong anti-interference ability for detecting tetracyclines, and is expected to be used for on-site qualitative identification of tetracyclines in the actual samples. Moreover, the sensing performance of the proposed fluorometric method was compared with the reported Eu3+-based method for detecting tetracyclines, and the proposed method showed a wide linear detection range with comparable detection limit, relative standard deviation (RSD), and recovery rate (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization and rapid detection of tetracyclines
ce:section.ce:para: The established sensor not only has high sensitivity and strong anti-interference ability, but also allows visual detection of tetracyclines. OTC was chosen as the model for tetracyclines to explore the paper sensors. As shown in Fig. 5a and b , the Eu-CDs test paper appeared white under daylight and blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. After adding different concentrations of OTC, the color of the test paper showed a significant change from blue, and pink to green-yellow (Fig. 5c). The reason for the test paper showing green-yellow fluorescence was explored. Filter paper not immersed in Eu-CDs solution showed a blue-purple under the UV lamp (Fig. 5d). The color of the filter paper showed an evident change from blue-purple to green after adding different concentrations of OTC with the pH 11(Fig. 5e), because the above Eu-CDs test paper was prepared at pH 11. This indicated that the green-yellow fluorescence of the Eu-CDs test paper was derived from the fluorescence of the OTC itself. The above result indicated that the Eu-CDs test paper has great potential for visual OTC detection.

Eu-CDs could be used as a ratiometric fluorescent probes for the visual detection of OTC (OTC was selected as the model of tetracyclines). However, visual detection methods with the naked eye does not enable on-site quantitative analysis of OTC. To solve the problem, we utilized a smartphone and a 365 nm UV lamp as the portable sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 6 , the smartphone with RGB Color Picker (color recognizer application) installed can convert the fluorescence colors into color information (RGB value). The red channel (R) and the green channel (G) were employed to calculate the RGB ratio. There was a good linear relationship between R/G and OTC concentration (0.00–140.88 μM), and the corresponding linear equation was R/G = 0.01713 [OTC] + 0.52415 (R = 0.9910). The lowest detectable concentration was 0.10 μM. It indicated that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform has great promising prospects for the real-time quantitative detection of tetracyclines.

In this work, the feasibility of the proposed method for the detection of tetracyclines was evaluated. The proposed method was compared with the Chinese national standard method for detecting tetracyclines. Firstly, known concentrations of OTC (OTC was selected as the model of tetracyclines) solutions were detected by the two methods mentioned above. The results revealed that the concentration of tetracycline detected by the two methods was generally consistent with the known concentrations of OTC (Fig. 7 a). Then, to investigate further the feasibility of the proposed method, the detection results of the above two methods were compared for the actual sample (chicken) spiked with the OTC standard solution (Fig. 7b), and the detection results of both methods were basically consistent. This showed that the proposed method had similar accuracy to the China national standard method. These also indicated that the proposed method was feasible for detecting tetracyclines.

Furthermore, the practicability of the sensor was evaluated, and the established sensor was applied to detect tetracyclines in actual samples (chicken, pork and fish). As shown in Table S2, the recovery rate (82.90–116.91%) and the RSD (0.06–2.05%) were satisfactory. This indicates that the established sensor could be used for detecting tetracyclines in actual samples.

The prepared Eu-CDs could be used for detecting tetracyclines by fluorometric and colorimetric methods. The sensing mechanism of sensor for tetracyclines was ascribed to the IFE, AE, and SQE. The established ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting tetracyclines has good sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy. Moreover, the proposed method has similar accuracy to the China national standard method, and the proposed method is feasible for detecting tetracyclines in actual samples. The potential application of the Eu-CDs test paper was explored, and the smartphone with RGB Color Picker was employed to analyze the concentration of tetracyclines with satisfactory results. Therefore, the proposed sensor based on Eu-CDs provides a convenient, rapid, visual, and reliable way for real-time monitoring of tetracyclines, which is expected to be applied in the fields of food safety and environmental protection.

Ya Jie Fan: Resources, Writing – original draft. Zhen Guang Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Ming Su: Data curation. Xiao Tong Liu: Resources. Shi Gang Shen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jiang Xue Dong: Methodology, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. B2019201397, B2015201193), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M670678), the Fundation for Central Government Guidance for Local Science and Technology Development of Hebei Province (No. 216Z2605G), Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Hebei Province- High Level Talent Team Construction Project (No. 225A4802D), Science and Technology Innovation Bureau of Baoding High-tech Zone Unveiled the Top Special Project (No. JBGS-2022-1-011), the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of Hebei University (No. 521000981136), Research Innovation Team of College of Chemistry and Materials Science of Hebei University (No. hxkytd-py2101).
